lines.text: Section nine. Tissue engineering and pattern formation — programming multicellular structure.
Many of the most striking biological behaviours emerge from collective coordination of many cells.

A multicellular organism arises from a single fertilised egg through patterns of cell division, differentiation, and positional choice that produce reliable structures across thousands of generations.
Reproducing this kind of patterned multicellular behaviour with engineered cells is one of the most ambitious goals in synthetic biology.

Traditional tissue engineering relies on extracellular scaffolds — three-dimensional matrices made of polymers or natural materials — that physically constrain where cells go and how they differentiate.
The scaffolds direct cellular organisation through top-down structural cues.

A synthetic biology approach adds bottom-up programming: cells themselves are engineered to communicate, sense their position relative to neighbours, and choose their behaviour based on what they sense.
Pattern formation through cell-to-cell signalling has been demonstrated in elegant proof-of-principle experiments.
One particularly clean example uses the quorum-sensing system from Vibrio cholerae to engineer pattern-forming cellular communities in E. coli.
The setup involves sender cells that produce a signalling molecule and receiver cells whose response depends on the local concentration of that signal.
Receiver cells were designed to act as band-pass filters — they only respond to a specific range of signalling concentrations, ignoring concentrations that are too low or too high.
Place a sender cell in the middle of a lawn of receiver cells, and the signal diffuses outward, creating a concentration gradient.
Receiver cells at the right distance from the sender — where the concentration falls in their bandpass range — fluoresce.
Receiver cells too close (saturated) or too far (sub-threshold) do not fluoresce.
The visible result is a ring of fluorescence around the sender cell — a bullseye pattern, emerged spontaneously from the engineered cellular logic.
By tuning the bandpass parameters and arranging multiple senders, you can engineer more complex patterns — ellipses, concentric circles, clovers.
Each pattern is the visible signature of the underlying cellular logic and signalling kinetics.
Another set of approaches uses light-driven patterning.

The bacterial camera we mentioned briefly in chapter five works by engineering E. coli that respond to red light using a chimeric photoreceptor system, where the light input controls expression of a chemical output.

When a high-contrast image is projected onto a lawn of these cells, the cells in lit regions and dark regions express different colours, capturing the image in living cells at resolutions exceeding 100 megapixels per square inch.
The image is recorded by the bacteria.

Extending this further, researchers built the edge-detector circuit we discussed in chapter five, which lets bacterial populations detect the boundaries between lit and dark regions of a projected image and produce visible pigment exactly at those boundaries.
This is genuinely multicellular image processing implemented in engineered cells.
The applications of programmable pattern formation are broad.
Patterned biomaterials — engineered tissues that lay themselves down in specified architectures.
Self-organising organoids — three-dimensional cellular structures that mimic specific organs and are useful for drug screening and disease modelling.
Parallel biological computation — using lawns of cells as computational substrates, each cell a tiny processor, the whole lawn a large parallel computer.
All of these are research-stage rather than commercial, but the principle has been demonstrated and the field is investing in scaling it up.
